title: Implementation of the kidney protection strategy in critically ill patients with acute kidney injury – a multi-center prospective cohort study
one_line_summary: This multicenter study assessed KDIGO Kidney Protection Strategy (KPS) adherence in 258 **critically** ill AKI patients, finding only 31% complete bundle implementation, yet this was linked to a six-fold higher renal recovery rate.
key_pearls: Complete KDIGO Kidney Protection Strategy (KPS) adherence was only 31% in **critically** ill AKI patients, highlighting a **significant** care gap.
Focus quality improvement on active KPS components: MAP optimization (adherence 32.9% for MAP >= **65 mmHg**) and hyperglycemia management (adherence 43.4% for blood glucose <= **180 mg**/dL).
Complete KPS adherence was independently associated with a 6.29-fold higher rate of renal recovery at hospital discharge (95% CI, 3.08-12.85).
Each additional KPS component implemented reduced the 30-day RRT incidence by 25% (adjusted SHR 0.75) and AKD beyond day 7 by 12% (adjusted SHR 0.88).
Consider EHR alerts and structured checklists to improve adherence to KPS components like hourly urine output and functional hemodynamic monitoring.
population: Critically ill adults (aged 18 years or older) with moderate or severe AKI (KDIGO stage 2 or 3) who required vasopressor support and/or mechanical ventilation at the time of enrollment.

sections.heading: Study Design & Patient Population
sections.content: This prospective, multicenter cohort study was conducted between July 2024 and December 2025 across five European academic medical centers (Münster and Gießen [Germany], Innsbruck [Austria], Nijmegen [The Netherlands], and Florence [Italy]). It included **critically** ill adults (aged 18 years or older) with moderate or severe AKI (KDIGO stage 2 or 3) who required vasopressor support and/or mechanical ventilation at enrollment. Exclusion criteria included CKD with eGFR < **20 mL**/min/1.73m2, chronic dialysis, renal transplantation, permanent renal artery ligation, AKI post-nephrectomy, or investigator/sponsor dependency. Baseline characteristics for the 258 patients showed a median age of 69 years (IQR 62-75), 65% male, with high illness severity (median SOFA 10, APACHE II 24). At enrollment, 64.6% required invasive mechanical ventilation. Common comorbidities included hypertension (58.5%), coronary artery disease (25.2%), and diabetes mellitus (25.2%). Median baseline eGFR was **68 mL**/min/1.73m2 (IQR 56-90).

sections.heading: Operationalization of the 7 Kidney Protection Strategy (KPS) Components
sections.content:
To evaluate adherence, KDIGO guidelines were operationalized into seven discrete, measurable components, initiated within 12 hours of AKI onset and sustained for 48 hours or until ICU discharge:
- Avoidance of Nephrotoxic Agents: Discontinuation unless absolutely necessary (e.g., specific antibiotics with no alternatives).
- Regular Functional Hemodynamic Monitoring: Assessment using echocardiography, transpulmonary thermodilution, or similar methods at least three times daily.
- Sustained Optimization of Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP): Avoidance of MAP < **65 mmHg**. Violations were defined as MAP < **65 mmHg** for >5 minutes (continuous monitoring) or two consecutive measurements < **65 mmHg** (non-continuous).
- Close Monitoring of Serum Creatinine: Daily measurement.
- Close Monitoring of Urine Output: Hourly documentation. A breach was a gap exceeding one consecutive hour.
- Avoidance of Hyperglycemia: Avoidance of blood glucose > **180 mg**/dL for longer than two consecutive measurements without corrective measures.
- Avoidance of Intravenous Radiocontrast Agents: No administration unless absolutely necessary for a life-threatening condition.

sections.heading: Adherence Rates & Care Gaps
sections.content:
Complete KPS bundle adherence was achieved in only 31.0% of patients (80/258; 95% CI, 25.5-37.2%). Component-specific adherence varied widely:
- Daily creatinine monitoring: 99.6% (257/258)
- Radiocontrast avoidance: 98.1% (253/258)
- Nephrotoxin management: 64.0% (165/258)
- Urine output monitoring: 62.4% (161/258)
- Hemodynamic monitoring: 58.9% (152/258)
- Hyperglycemia management: 43.4% (112/258)
- Optimization of MAP: 32.9% (85/258)
While overall complete KPS adherence was relatively homogeneous across larger centers (26% to 35%), component-level variation was vast. For instance, urine output monitoring violations ranged from 2% to 72% and MAP optimization violations from 20% to 72% across centers, indicating **significant** organizational and workflow differences.

sections.heading: Clinical Outcomes & Competing-Risk Analyses
sections.content: In-hospital **mortality** was substantial (30-day **mortality** 16%; 90-day **mortality** 28%), acting as a major competing risk for renal outcomes. Fine-Gray subdistribution hazard regression models were used to account for death as a competing event. Acute Kidney Disease (AKD) beyond Day 7 was present in 59% of patients. Complete KPS adherence was associated with a lower incidence of AKD in univariable analysis (SHR 0.64; 95% CI, 0.41-0.99; p = 0.046), but this became non-**significant** after multivariable adjustment (adjusted SHR 0.71; 95% CI, 0.42-1.20; p = 0.19). Renal recovery at hospital discharge was documented in 58% of patients. Complete KPS adherence was strongly and independently associated with a higher rate of renal recovery in both univariable (SHR 6.02; 95% CI, 4.00-9.05; p < 0.0001) and multivariable adjusted models (adjusted SHR 6.29; 95% CI, 3.08-12.85; p < 0.0001). Renal Replacement Therapy (RRT) within 30 days was required by 30.5% of patients. Complete KPS adherence was associated with a lower incidence of RRT within 30 days in univariable analysis (SHR 0.12; 95% CI, 0.02-0.91; p = 0.040) and remained directionally consistent but borderline non-**significant** after adjustment (adjusted SHR 0.16; 95% CI, 0.02-1.11; p = 0.064).

sections.heading: Dose-Response Relationship
sections.content: Linear and restricted cubic spline Fine-Gray models demonstrated a clear dose-response relationship between the number of KPS components fulfilled (0 to 7) and renal outcomes. Each additional KPS component implemented was associated with a lower cumulative incidence of RRT within 30 days (adjusted SHR 0.75; 95% CI, 0.59-0.97) and a lower incidence of AKD beyond day 7 (adjusted SHR 0.88; 95% CI, 0.77-0.99). Spline models revealed **significant** non-linearity for renal recovery (p < 0.001) and RRT within 30 days (p = 0.014), indicating that incremental improvements in bundle compliance (even if incomplete) yield progressive clinical benefits.

strengths_limitations: Strengths include its prospective, multicenter, multinational design, rigorous operationalization of KDIGO guidelines, and robust Fine-Gray competing-risk **survival** analysis. Limitations are its observational design preventing causal inference, potential for confounding and bias, binary assessment of KPS components, and a small sample size at one center.
